title: Hedgerow and tree poisonous plants
subtitle: A reference guide to the hedgerow and garden plants most dangerous to livestock.
excerpt: Most livestock poisoning incidents involve plants that are readily accessible; hedge clippings thrown into a paddock, a fallen yew branch, access to a garden during drought. The list of dangerous plants is manageable. Learning the main species prevents most incidents.
sourceNotes: DEFRA, poisonous plants guidance for livestock keepers. Animal Poison Line, plant toxicity fact sheets. AHDB, pasture weed and poisonous plant management.

glossaryTerms.definition: The alkaloid toxin in yew (Taxus baccata). Present in all parts of the plant except the red berry flesh. Acts on the heart's electrical conduction system and can cause sudden death in livestock with no prior warning signs. Yew toxicity is dose-dependent and death can occur with a relatively small quantity of leaf or bark. All livestock species are susceptible.

A common yellow-flowered native plant found on roadsides, rough pasture, and disturbed ground. Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids that cause progressive, irreversible liver damage in horses, cattle, and sheep. Livestock typically avoid growing ragwort but will eat wilted or dried ragwort in hay, when the palatability-warning smell is masked. Under the Ragwort Control Act 2003, landowners near land used by horses must manage ragwort spread.

content.text: The highest-risk scenarios for livestock poisoning are: prunings or clippings thrown into the field, access to garden areas during dry weather when pasture is poor, and ragwort or other poisonous plants present in bought hay. Healthy pasture with adequate grazing usually means livestock avoid toxic plants by preference; hungry animals on poor grazing are at far higher risk.
Yew (Taxus baccata) is the most acutely dangerous plant in the UK hedgerow context. The

alkaloids cause cardiac arrest with no prior clinical signs in many cases; a cow may be found dead with no history of apparent illness. Never allow yew hedge clippings to enter any field used by livestock. Remove or fence off any yew growing adjacent to livestock paddocks.

causes cumulative liver damage, and clinical signs (weight loss, jaundice, photosensitisation) only appear once a significant portion of the liver is already damaged; by which point the damage is irreversible. Remove ragwort from pasture before it flowers and sets seed. Do not compost pulled ragwort in a pile adjacent to the field.

Rhododendron (and related Azalea species) contain grayanotoxins that cause vomiting, salivation, and in severe cases heart rhythm disturbance in sheep and cattle. Goats are particularly susceptible. Common in hedgerows and garden boundaries in acidic soil areas. Remove all rhododendron from boundaries accessible to stock.

Laburnum seed pods are toxic to all livestock, causing seizures and respiratory distress. Do not grow laburnum adjacent to livestock fields. Bracken fern (Pteridium aquilinum) contains thiaminase and carcinogenic compounds. Cattle and horses are most commonly affected by bracken poisoning. Manage bracken by repeated cutting in summer; it weakens over several seasons if cut before sporing.
